text: Unsolved black-hole problem #1: Spacetime singularities & their quantum-gravity resolution. General relativity predicts that gravitational collapse produces a spacetime singularity where curvature invariants diverge and the classical theory breaks down. The Penrose-Hawking theorems prove such singularities are generic (not artifacts of symmetry), given reasonable energy and causality conditions. What physically replaces the singularity is unknown and is believed to require a quantum theory of gravity, which does not yet exist in complete, predictive form. First posed 1916-1965 by Karl Schwarzschild, Roger Penrose, Stephen Hawking. Status: open. Key ideas: Curvature singularities are where geodesics are incomplete and invariants like the Kretschmann scalar diverge.; Penrose's trapped-surface argument makes singularity formation generic, independent of exact symmetry.; Cosmic censorship (problems #04, #05) concerns whether such singularities are hidden behind horizons.; Candidate resolutions replace r=0 with a quantum-gravity core: a bounce (loop quantum gravity), a fuzzball/string condensate, or a regular (non-singular) black-hole interior.; Any resolution must reduce to classical GR in the appropriate limit and respect the singularity theorems' assumptions or evade one of them (e.g. via quantum energy-condition violation)..
title: Spacetime singularities & their quantum-gravity resolution

text: On the Gravitational Field of a Mass Point According to Einstein's Theory (1916) by Karl Schwarzschild — foundational reference for black-hole problem #1 (Spacetime singularities & their quantum-gravity resolution). Sitzungsber. Preuss. Akad. Wiss.; the Schwarzschild solution.

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #2: The black-hole information paradox. Hawking's 1975 calculation shows a black hole emits thermal radiation and slowly evaporates. If the radiation is exactly thermal and the hole disappears completely, an initial pure quantum state evolves into a mixed (thermal) state, violating the unitarity of quantum mechanics and destroying information. Reconciling black-hole evaporation with unitarity is the central problem of quantum gravity's low-energy regime. Recent island/replica-wormhole computations reproduce a unitary Page curve for the radiation entropy, but the explicit mechanism by which information escapes the interior remains open. First posed 1975-1976 by Stephen Hawking. Status: open-with-recent-progress. Key ideas: Hawking radiation is (to leading semiclassical order) thermal, carrying no information about what formed the hole.; The Page curve: if evaporation is unitary, the radiation's entanglement entropy must rise then fall, peaking at the Page time (~half the entropy radiated).; Islands / quantum extremal surfaces: regions of the interior counted as part of the radiation's entropy, reproducing the Page curve from a gravitational computation.; Replica wormholes: subleading gravitational saddles in the Euclidean path integral that supply the entropy turnover.; Tension with the equivalence principle is sharpened by the firewall argument (problem #06).; Open core: even with a unitary Page curve, the physical mechanism transferring information from interior to radiation (and the fate of the interior) is not settled..
title: The black-hole information paradox

text: Particle Creation by Black Holes (1975) by Stephen Hawking — foundational reference for black-hole problem #2 (The black-hole information paradox). Commun. Math. Phys. 43, 199 — the Hawking-radiation derivation.

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #3: Microscopic origin of Bekenstein-Hawking entropy. A black hole carries entropy S = k_B c^3 A / (4 G hbar) — one quarter of its horizon area in Planck units (Bekenstein 1973; Hawking 1975). In ordinary statistical mechanics, entropy counts microstates. What are the microstates of a black hole? String theory (Strominger-Vafa 1996) reproduced S = A/4 by counting D-brane states for special supersymmetric, extremal (and near-extremal) black holes. A microscopic state-counting for a generic astrophysical (e.g. Kerr) black hole, and a framework-independent understanding of why the answer is universally A/4, remain open. First posed 1972-1973 by Jacob Bekenstein, Stephen Hawking. Status: partially-solved. Key ideas: S = A/4 in Planck units is universal across black-hole types — a strong hint about quantum gravity.; The area law motivated the holographic principle ('t Hooft, Susskind): degrees of freedom scale with area, not volume.; Strominger-Vafa counted Bogomol'nyi-Prasad-Sommerfield (BPS) D-brane microstates and matched A/4 exactly for extremal charged black holes.; Loop quantum gravity counts spin-network punctures of the horizon, fixing A/4 via the Barbero-Immirzi parameter.; Logarithmic corrections to S (Sen and others) provide cross-checks between approaches.; Open: a microscopic count for a generic (non-extremal, rotating, astrophysical) black hole and a framework-independent derivation of the 1/4..
title: Microscopic origin of Bekenstein-Hawking entropy

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #4: Strong cosmic censorship & the Cauchy horizon. Inside charged (Reissner-Nordstrom) and rotating (Kerr) black holes, general relativity's equations lose predictive power at an inner 'Cauchy horizon' beyond which the future is not determined by initial data. Strong cosmic censorship (Penrose 1969) conjectures that, for generic initial data, the Cauchy horizon is unstable and becomes a singular boundary, so determinism is preserved (no observer can pass into the unpredictable region). Whether this holds is open: rigorous work supports a (weaker, Christodoulou) formulation in many cases, but near-extremal Reissner-Nordstrom-de Sitter black holes provide apparent counterexamples. First posed 1969 by Roger Penrose. Status: open-with-recent-progress. Key ideas: The Cauchy horizon is where predictability from initial data ends; classical GR cannot say what lies beyond.; Blueshift instability: perturbations falling in are infinitely blueshifted at the Cauchy horizon, suggesting it becomes singular (Simpson-Penrose).; Mass inflation (Poisson-Israel): the interior mass function diverges, reinforcing a singular Cauchy horizon.; Christodoulou's reformulation: the question is the regularity (differentiability) of the metric extension across the horizon, not mere continuity.; Quasinormal-mode decay rates vs blueshift growth set whether perturbations remain regular; near-extremal Reissner-Nordstrom-de Sitter can violate the expected censorship.; Outcome bears on whether GR is a deterministic theory in the strong-field interior..
title: Strong cosmic censorship & the Cauchy horizon

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #5: Weak cosmic censorship. Weak cosmic censorship (Penrose 1969) conjectures that the singularities produced by realistic gravitational collapse are always hidden behind event horizons, so no 'naked' singularity is visible to a distant observer and predictability at infinity is preserved. There is no general proof. Spherically-symmetric counterexamples exist (Christodoulou) but appear non-generic; gedanken experiments that try to over-spin or over-charge a black hole into a naked singularity are generically thwarted, though with subtle near-extremal cases; and clean violations are known in asymptotically anti-de Sitter spacetimes. First posed 1969 by Roger Penrose. Status: open-with-recent-progress. Key ideas: A naked singularity would emit unpredictable signals to infinity, breaking the predictive power of GR for outside observers.; Christodoulou proved naked singularities CAN form in spherical scalar-field collapse, then proved they are non-generic (perturbations remove them).; Critical collapse (Choptuik): at the precise threshold between dispersal and black-hole formation sits a self-similar, marginally-naked solution.; Overspinning/overcharging gedanken experiments: attempts to push a black hole past extremality (a>M) by throwing in matter are generically prevented, with delicate near-extremal exceptions.; In asymptotically anti-de Sitter space, explicit WCC violations are known (Horowitz, Santos and collaborators).; Closely tied to whether the final state of collapse is always a Kerr black hole..
title: Weak cosmic censorship

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #6: The firewall paradox (AMPS). Almheiri, Marolf, Polchinski and Sully (2012) argued that three widely-held assumptions cannot all be true for an old (post-Page-time) black hole: (i) evaporation is unitary, (ii) effective field theory is valid outside the horizon, and (iii) an infalling observer sees nothing special at the horizon (the equivalence principle / 'no drama'). Monogamy of entanglement forces a contradiction; the proposed escape is that the horizon is replaced by a high-energy 'firewall' that destroys infalling observers, violating the equivalence principle. Whether firewalls exist, or which assumption truly fails, is open. First posed 2012 by Ahmed Almheiri, Donald Marolf, Joseph Polchinski, James Sully. Status: open. Key ideas: Monogamy of entanglement: a late Hawking quantum cannot be maximally entangled with both its early-radiation partner (required by unitarity) and its interior partner (required by a smooth horizon).; Page time: the argument bites only for 'old' black holes that have radiated more than half their entropy.; Firewall: dropping the smooth-horizon assumption replaces the horizon with a wall of high-energy quanta.; Mathur's earlier 'small corrections' theorem reached a related conclusion via fuzzballs.; ER=EPR (problem #08) was proposed partly to dissolve the firewall by identifying the two entanglements geometrically.; The island/replica resolution of #02 bears directly on whether a firewall is required..
title: The firewall paradox (AMPS)

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #7: Black-hole complementarity & its consistency. Black-hole complementarity (Susskind, Thorlacius, Uglum 1993; 't Hooft) proposes that the descriptions seen by an infalling observer (who passes a smooth horizon) and an external observer (who sees information thermalized and re-emitted on a 'stretched horizon') are complementary, not contradictory, because no single observer can access both. It was the leading reconciliation of unitarity with a smooth horizon until the firewall argument (#06) challenged its consistency. Whether complementarity is internally consistent, and how it is realized, is open. First posed 1993 by Leonard Susskind, Larus Thorlacius, John Uglum, Gerard 't Hooft. Status: contested. Key ideas: No-cloning is respected because no observer sees information both inside and in the radiation.; The 'stretched horizon' is a fictitious membrane (a Planck length outside the true horizon) that absorbs, thermalizes and re-radiates information for the external observer.; Complementarity underlies and motivated the holographic principle.; Hayden-Preskill: a black hole that has already radiated half its entropy returns infalling information very quickly (the black hole as a near-optimal scrambler).; The firewall argument (#06) claims complementarity is inconsistent for old black holes; ER=EPR (#08) is a proposed rescue.; AdS/CFT gives a manifestly unitary external description, sharpening the question of how the interior is encoded..
title: Black-hole complementarity & its consistency

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #8: ER=EPR and the entanglement-geometry connection. Maldacena and Susskind (2013) conjectured ER=EPR: that an Einstein-Rosen bridge (ER, a wormhole) is dual to quantum entanglement (EPR), so two entangled systems are connected by a (generally non-traversable) geometric bridge. More broadly, the conjecture suggests spacetime connectivity itself emerges from entanglement (Van Raamsdonk; Ryu-Takayanagi). A precise, general, and proven formulation — specifying exactly which entangled states correspond to which geometries, and in what regime — remains open. First posed 2013 by Juan Maldacena, Leonard Susskind. Status: open. Key ideas: An eternal AdS black hole is dual to two CFTs in the thermofield-double (maximally entangled) state, connected by a non-traversable wormhole (Maldacena 2001).; ER=EPR generalizes this: entanglement between any two systems is geometric connectivity.; Van Raamsdonk: disentangling boundary regions pinches off the bulk, so entanglement 'builds' spacetime.; Ryu-Takayanagi: entanglement entropy equals the area of a bulk minimal surface — area/entanglement again.; Traversable wormholes (Gao-Jafferis-Wall) realize controlled information transfer, sharpening ER=EPR.; Bears on the firewall (#06): identifying interior and exterior partners as the same wormhole endpoints can preserve a smooth horizon..
title: ER=EPR and the entanglement-geometry connection

text: The Large N Limit of Superconformal Field Theories and Supergravity (AdS/CFT) (1998) by Juan Maldacena — foundational reference for black-hole problem #8 (ER=EPR and the entanglement-geometry connection). hep-th/9711200

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #9: The endpoint of evaporation: remnants vs information release. As a black hole evaporates via Hawking radiation it shrinks toward the Planck scale, where the semiclassical description fails. What is the final state? Candidates include: a stable or long-lived Planck-mass remnant retaining the lost information; complete evaporation with information carried out in subtle correlations of the radiation; information stored in a disconnected 'baby universe'; or a genuine breakdown of unitary evolution. Each option has serious difficulties (e.g. remnants must carry unboundedly large entropy for a given mass, threatening infinite pair-production), and none is established. First posed 1974-1992 by Stephen Hawking, Steven Giddings. Status: open. Key ideas: Semiclassical gravity breaks down once the hole reaches the Planck mass (~10^-8 kg), so the true endpoint needs quantum gravity.; Stable remnant: a Planck-scale object stores the information — but must have enormous degeneracy at fixed mass, risking divergent pair-production (the 'remnant problem').; Complete evaporation + information in correlations: requires the late radiation to be highly non-thermal in a way the leading calculation misses (now supported by the island/Page-curve results, #02).; Baby universe: information escapes into a causally-disconnected region (does not help an outside observer recover it).; Long-lived (metastable) remnant as a phenomenological middle ground.; The answer is constrained by, and feeds back into, the information paradox and its island resolution..
title: The endpoint of evaporation: remnants vs information release

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #10: The trans-Planckian problem of Hawking radiation. In the standard derivation, the Hawking quanta seen at late times are traced back to modes that were, near the horizon at early times, blueshifted to arbitrarily high (trans-Planckian) frequencies — far beyond where the underlying field theory and the fixed-background approximation can be trusted. The trans-Planckian problem asks whether Hawking radiation is a genuine, robust prediction or an artifact of extrapolating physics into an unknown ultraviolet regime. Analogue-gravity systems and modified-dispersion models give strong evidence for robustness, but a fully satisfactory, first-principles demonstration awaits the true short-distance theory. First posed 1981-1991 by William Unruh, Ted Jacobson. Status: open-with-recent-progress. Key ideas: Near-horizon exponential blueshift sends the origin of late Hawking quanta to trans-Planckian frequencies in the naive calculation.; Modified-dispersion-relation models (introducing a UV cutoff) still reproduce a thermal spectrum to good approximation — evidence of robustness.; Analogue gravity: sonic/optical/BEC 'horizons' realize the same kinematics with KNOWN microphysics, providing experimental tests.; Observation of stimulated and (claimed) spontaneous analogue Hawking radiation in BECs (Steinhauer) supports the effect's robustness.; The result hints that Hawking radiation is a low-energy, horizon-kinematics phenomenon insensitive to UV details — but this is argued, not proven.; Connects to whether the equivalence principle plus QFT alone fix the radiation, independent of quantum gravity..
title: The trans-Planckian problem of Hawking radiation

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #11: Testing the no-hair theorem & exotic compact objects. The no-hair theorems (Israel, Carter, Hawking, Robinson) state that a stationary, isolated black hole in general relativity is completely characterized by mass, spin, and charge — all higher multipole moments are fixed functions of these (the Kerr metric for the astrophysical, uncharged case). The open question is observational and theoretical: are the compact objects we detect actually Kerr black holes, or could they be exotic alternatives (boson stars, gravastars, wormholes, fuzzballs) or harbor deviations from GR? Black-hole spectroscopy (quasinormal-mode ringdown with LIGO/Virgo/KAGRA) and the Event Horizon Telescope shadow are now testing this directly, with results so far consistent with Kerr but far from a decisive null. First posed 1967-1975 (theorems); 2015- (precision tests) by Werner Israel, Brandon Carter, Stephen Hawking, David Robinson. Status: open-observational. Key ideas: No-hair: (mass, spin, charge) fix all multipoles; e.g. the quadrupole is Q = -J^2/M for Kerr.; Quasinormal modes: the ringdown after a merger is a superposition of damped tones whose frequencies depend ONLY on the remnant mass and spin if it is Kerr.; Black-hole spectroscopy: detecting two or more QNMs over-determines (M, J) and tests the Kerr prediction; a mismatch would signal new physics or an exotic object.; The EHT shadow size/shape tests the photon-region geometry against Kerr.; Exotic compact objects (boson stars, gravastars, wormholes) can mimic some signatures but typically differ in ringdown 'echoes' or shadow structure.; Charge is astrophysically negligible, so the relevant test is two-parameter Kerr..
title: Testing the no-hair theorem & exotic compact objects

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #12: Origin & seeds of supermassive black holes. Quasars powered by billion-solar-mass black holes are observed at redshift z > 7, less than ~700 million years after the Big Bang. Growing such masses so quickly from ordinary stellar-mass seeds via Eddington-limited accretion is difficult. The open problem is the formation channel: light seeds (~100 M_sun remnants of Population III stars) with episodes of super-Eddington accretion, heavy seeds (~10^4-10^6 M_sun) from direct collapse of pristine gas clouds, or runaway collapse in dense nuclear star clusters — and which dominates. It is an astrophysical problem (no new fundamental physics required), now sharply constrained by JWST and high-z quasar surveys. First posed 2001-present (sharpened by high-z discoveries) by Martin Rees, Marta Volonteri. Status: open-observational. Key ideas: e-folding (Salpeter) time for Eddington-limited growth is ~45 Myr; reaching 10^9 M_sun by z~7 from a 100 M_sun seed requires near-continuous accretion or super-Eddington phases.; Light seeds: Population III stellar remnants (~10-1000 M_sun).; Heavy seeds: direct collapse of atomically-cooling, metal-free gas to ~10^4-10^6 M_sun, avoiding fragmentation (needs Lyman-Werner radiation to suppress H2 cooling).; Runaway stellar collisions in dense nuclear clusters as an intermediate channel.; JWST is finding abundant faint AGN / overmassive black holes at high z, reshaping the seed debate.; Connects to intermediate-mass black holes (#14) as possible leftover seeds..
title: Origin & seeds of supermassive black holes

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #13: The final-parsec problem. When two galaxies merge, their central supermassive black holes sink toward the center and form a binary. Dynamical friction and stellar scattering harden the binary efficiently down to roughly parsec separation, but there the binary may run out of stars on intersecting (loss-cone) orbits to extract its angular momentum, while gravitational-wave emission is still far too weak to drive a merger. The binary could then stall for longer than a Hubble time. Whether (and how) SMBH binaries cross this 'final parsec' to merge is open — and directly relevant to the nanohertz gravitational-wave background now detected by pulsar-timing arrays. First posed 1980 by Mitchell Begelman, Roger Blandford, Martin Rees. Status: open-with-recent-progress. Key ideas: Stages: dynamical friction (kpc) -> three-body stellar scattering (parsec) -> gravitational-wave inspiral (milliparsec). The gap is between the second and third.; Loss-cone depletion: stars on orbits that interact with the binary are ejected faster than they are replenished, stalling the hardening.; Proposed solutions: non-spherical (triaxial) or rotating nuclei keep the loss cone full; gas-disk torques; or a third black hole inducing Kozai-Lidov oscillations.; The detected PTA nanohertz GW background is consistent with a cosmic population of merging SMBH binaries, evidence that the final parsec IS crossed in many systems.; Sets the event rate for future space-based (LISA) and PTA gravitational-wave observations.; Connects to SMBH seeds (#12) and galaxy-merger demographics..
title: The final-parsec problem

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #14: Intermediate-mass black holes: existence & formation. Black holes are well established in two regimes: stellar-mass (~3-100 M_sun, from stellar collapse) and supermassive (~10^6-10^10 M_sun, in galactic nuclei). Intermediate-mass black holes (IMBHs, ~100-10^5 M_sun) are the expected bridge, but observational confirmation is sparse and their formation channels uncertain. The open problem: do IMBHs exist as a genuine population, where (globular clusters, dwarf-galaxy nuclei, ultraluminous X-ray sources?), and how do they form (runaway stellar collisions, repeated mergers, leftover SMBH seeds)? Gravitational-wave events like GW190521 (a ~150 M_sun remnant) and dynamical/X-ray candidates are narrowing the gap. First posed 1970s-present by (community). Status: open-observational. Key ideas: Candidate sites: cores of globular and nuclear star clusters; nuclei of dwarf galaxies; some ultraluminous X-ray sources (ULXs).; Formation channels: runaway collisions of massive stars in dense clusters; hierarchical (repeated) mergers of stellar-mass black holes; surviving direct-collapse SMBH seeds.; GW190521 produced a ~150 M_sun remnant — the first fairly secure IMBH-scale object — and its components fall in/near the pair-instability gap (#16).; Dynamical mass measurements in clusters are contentious (alternatives: a dense subcluster of stellar remnants can mimic an IMBH).; An IMBH population would test SMBH seed scenarios (#12) and hierarchical-merger predictions.; Future facilities (LISA, ngVLA, next-gen X-ray) target the IMBH regime directly..
title: Intermediate-mass black holes: existence & formation

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #15: Primordial black holes as dark matter. Black holes could have formed in the very early universe from the collapse of large density fluctuations, before stars existed (Hawking 1971; Carr-Hawking 1974). Such primordial black holes (PBHs) are a candidate for some or all of the dark matter, requiring no new particle. Observational limits (Hawking-evaporation constraints, microlensing, dynamical and accretion bounds, gravitational waves) exclude PBHs as the dominant dark matter across most mass ranges, but an 'asteroid-mass' window (~10^17-10^23 g) remains largely open, and PBHs could still be a sub-component or seed SMBHs. Whether PBHs exist at all, and their cosmological role, is unresolved. First posed 1971-1974 by Stephen Hawking, Bernard Carr. Status: open-observational. Key ideas: PBHs form when a sufficiently large primordial overdensity re-enters the horizon and collapses; the mass is set by the horizon mass at that epoch.; Mass spectrum is broad in principle (from sub-gram to supermassive), so constraints are window-by-window.; Hawking evaporation: PBHs below ~5e14 g would have evaporated by now; their final bursts are searched for (gamma rays).; Microlensing (MACHO/EROS/OGLE/Subaru-HSC), dynamical heating of star clusters, CMB accretion, and the LIGO/Virgo merger population all set bounds.; The asteroid-mass window (~10^17-10^23 g) is hard to constrain (lensing wave-optics + finite-source effects) and remains viable for 100% dark matter.; PBHs could also be sub-dominant yet seed supermassive black holes (#12) or contribute to GW mergers..
title: Primordial black holes as dark matter

text: Unsolved black-hole problem #16: The compact-object mass gaps. Two apparent gaps in the compact-object mass distribution are unexplained. The LOWER mass gap (~3-5 M_sun) lies between the heaviest neutron stars and the lightest black holes; X-ray binary data once suggested a real dearth there, but gravitational-wave events (e.g. GW190814's ~2.6 M_sun secondary, GW190425) populate or blur it. The UPPER (pair-instability) gap (~50-120 M_sun) is predicted because pair-instability supernovae leave no remnant; yet GW190521's ~85 M_sun component sits inside it. The open problem: are these gaps real features of stellar/supernova physics, or artifacts of selection and small samples — and what fills them (hierarchical mergers, accretion, exotic channels)? First posed 1990s-present by (community). Status: open-observational. Key ideas: Lower gap (~3-5 M_sun): X-ray binaries showed few objects there; whether it is a true gap depends on the supernova explosion mechanism (rapid vs delayed).; GW190814's secondary (~2.6 M_sun) is right in the lower gap — neutron star or black hole is unknown.; Upper (pair-instability) gap (~50-120 M_sun): electron-positron pair production in very massive stellar cores triggers an instability that leaves NO remnant, forbidding direct-collapse BHs in this range.; GW190521's ~85 M_sun component lies inside the upper gap, suggesting hierarchical (second-generation) mergers or uncertain pair-instability boundaries.; Selection effects and small samples can mimic or hide a gap; population inference is improving with each LIGO/Virgo/KAGRA run.; Resolving the gaps constrains supernova physics, the maximum neutron-star mass, and binary-evolution channels..
title: The compact-object mass gaps
